coredata.dc:title: Low-temperature rapid synthesis of high-stable carbon dots and its application in biochemical sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Caicheng
Qing, Taiping
Fu, Qing
Jiang, Zixin
Xu, Jin
Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have wide applications in chemical sensor, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis, but most preparations of carbon dots need high temperature and long reaction time. In this work, a chemical oxidation and surface passivation approach was introduced to synthesize fluorescent CDs in low-temperature condition using phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide as the oxidant and passivator, respectively. The as-prepared CDs exhibited two emission bands at 356 nm and 440 nm with different excited wavelengths, and high quantum yield (25.51%). More important, the fluorescent CDs display good water-solubility and high biocompatibility even under broad pH range or high ionic strengths. Moreover, this high-stable fluorescent CDs was also employed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid sensing based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between ascorbic acid and CDs. This strategy represented excellent sensitivity and selectivity for ascorbic acid analysis both in buffer and biological samples, providing a valuable platform for ascorbic acid detection in clinic diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Low-temperature
High-stability
Ratiometric sensing
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ascorbic acid detection based on fluorescent CDs
Fluorescent response of CDs toward ascorbic acid
Detection of ascorbic acid in complex sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of different reaction conditions: a: sucrose; b: sucrose + ammonium hydroxide; c: sucrose + phosphoric acid; d: sucrose + phosphoric acid + ammonium hydroxide (a) Emission spectrum of CDs with Ex = 280 nm. Inset shows CDs under 254 nm UV light. (b) Emission spectrum of CDs with Ex = 340 nm. Inset shows CDs under 310 nm UV light.

Basic characterizations of CDs. (a) and (b) HRTEM images of CDs. Insert: particle size distribution. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (d) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. (e) N 1s XPS spectrum. (f) P 2p XPS spectrum.

(a) and (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CDs, (Ex/Em = 280/356 nm) or (Ex/Em = 340/440 nm). Insert: emission photograph of CDs solutions under the visible light (left) and UV lamp (right). (c) 2D-countur plots of excitation/emission spectra of the CDs. (d) Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Stability of as-synthesized CDs. effect of various metal ions (100 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs at Em = 356 nm (a) and Em = 440 nm (b). (c) Fluorescent time scan of CDs for 2 h under 280 nm and 340 nm excitation. (d) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in different time after synthesized.
Cytotoxicity investigation of CDs. Cell viability of MCF-7 cells incubated with different CDs for 24 h (a) or 48 h (b).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions with AA concentration from 0 to 700 μM at Em = 356 nm (a) and Em = 440 nm (b), respectively. (c) Plot of F356/F440 versus AA concentration; Inset shows the linear relationship of F356/F440 versus AA concentration. (d) Selectivity studies of the CDs in the presence of interferences.

(a) The quenching efficiency (F/F0) of CDs with different AA concentration. The black curve was observed values, the red curve was corrected values, and the inset is the parameters used in Eq (2). (b) The fluorescent decay curves of the CDs in the absence (black curve) or presence (red curve) of AA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Schematic diagram for synthesizing CDs in low-temperature condition; (b) the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs with ascorbic acid.
Recovery rate of AA in the human serum.

head.ce:title: Low-temperature rapid synthesis of high-stable carbon dots and its application in biochemical sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, 411105, Hunan Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, 411105, Hunan Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have wide applications in chemical sensor, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis, but most preparations of carbon dots need high temperature and long reaction time. In this work, a chemical oxidation and surface passivation approach was introduced to synthesize fluorescent CDs in low-temperature condition using phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide as the oxidant and passivator, respectively. The as-prepared CDs exhibited two emission bands at 356 nm and 440 nm with different excited wavelengths, and high quantum yield (25.51%). More important, the fluorescent CDs display good water-solubility and high biocompatibility even under broad pH range or high ionic strengths. Moreover, this high-stable fluorescent CDs was also employed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid sensing based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between ascorbic acid and CDs. This strategy represented excellent sensitivity and selectivity for ascorbic acid analysis both in buffer and biological samples, providing a valuable platform for ascorbic acid detection in clinic diagnosis.

Schematic presentation of high-stable carbon dots (CDs) synthesis via chemical oxidation and surface passivation in low-temperature (80 °C). This excellent CDs was employed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid detection due to the inner filter effect between ascorbic acid and CDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of nanomaterials in the nanocarbon family, have attracted numerous attention because of their outstanding optical and chemical properties [1]. Comparing with the traditional fluorescent materials, fluorescent CDs are superior in the aspects of high aqueous solubility, distinguished photoluminescence properties, good biocompatibility, and tunable surface functionalities [2,3]. As a result, CDs are widely applied in the fields of nanomedicine, chemical sensor, electrocatalysis, and peroxidase mimetics [4–7]. Since the first successful obtained CDs after separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Xu’ group in 2004 [8], various synthesis methods for CDs have been studied and developed. According to the species of sources and products, these synthesis methods are mainly divided into top-down and bottom-up routes [9]. In top-down methods, large carbon nanomaterials (graphite and carbon nanotubes, etc.) are transformed into quantum-sized and fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (carbon quantum dots or carbon dots) by laser ablation, arc discharge, chemical, or electrochemical oxidation [10–13]. In bottom-up methods, the CDs are obtained from carbonization small molecules precursors by hydrothermal methods, microwave-assisted synthesis, pyrolysis or carbonization of precursors [14–19]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal synthesis is a popular method due to the simple synthesis process and large instruments independent. However, most of hydrothermal methods for CDs need high temperature (higher than 180 °C) and long reaction time (over 8 h), which limits their large-scale preparation and wider applications [20–23]. Thus, the development of facile and rapid synthesis methods is urgent and necessary for CDs’ further application.

Chemical oxidation is an effective approach to regulate the surface chemical and photoelectric properties of nanomaterials [24–26]. In 2008, Galiotis and co-workers prepared the single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with amino functional groups by acid oxidation [26]. Owing to the similar structure of CNTs and other carbon nanomaterials, the chemical oxidation strategy has also been used to regulate the photoelectric properties of other carbon nanomaterials. For instance, Peng et al. obtained luminescent carbogenic dots using concentrated sulfuric acid as the oxidizing agent. First, concentrated sulfuric acid dehydrated the carbohydrates to produce carbonaceous materials. Then, the acquired carbonaceous materials were disintegrated to individual carbogenic nanoparticles in presence of nitric acid. Finally, the carbogenic nanoparticles were changed into luminescent carbogenic dots by treated with passivator [27]. Moreover, chemical oxidation is also used to prepare carbon dots (CDs) from top to bottom [28–30]. The chemical oxidation method can provide simple route to synthesis different carbon nanomaterials, but the quantum yields of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials need further improvement [30]. More recently, surface passivation is an alternative strategy to improve synthetic process and fluorescence property of CDs. For example, the non-luminescent carbon nanoparticles can change into fluorescent CDs after treatment with 4,7,10- trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (passivator) at 120 °C for 72 h under nitrogen [27]. Besides, doping with heteroatoms can also significantly increase the quantum yield of CDs [31]. Zhu et al. reported a rapid and high-product method allows the manufacture of nitrogen-doped CDs, and the quantum yields up to ca. 80% [32]. Consequently, effective surface passivation and doping with heteroatoms are essential steps in order to prepare CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield.

Inspired by this, we hypothesized that chemical oxidation could also be applied to the preparation of CDs in bottom-up approaches. To testify this, a hydrothermal and oxidation approach was introduced to synthesis fluorescent CDs in low-temperature condition (80 °C). As shown in Scheme 1 , the sucrose and phosphoric acid was used as the carbon source and oxidant, respectively. Firstly, sucrose and phosphoric acid were mixed thoroughly and heated at 80 °C for 0.8 h in thermostat water bath. Then, the carbonaceous material was further passivated with ammonium hydroxide at room template. Finally, the fluorescent N/P codoped CDs could be observed. The as-prepared CDs show two emission bands at 356 and 440 nm with different excited wavelengths. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs at 356 nm can be suppressed by AA attributing to the inner filter effect (IFE). So, this high-stable fluorescent CDs was also employed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid sensing.

Ascorbic acid (AA), sucrose, ammonium hydroxide, phosphoric acid, and salt reagents (CuSO4, HgSO4, Fe(NO3)3, BaCl2, CoCl2, ZnSO4, MgSO4, AgNO3, Na2CO3, Pb(NO3)2, LiNO3, CaCl2, MnSO4, Ni(NO3)2, KNO3, Cd(NO3)2) were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA), Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), glutathione (GSH), haemoglobin (Hb), albumin from bovine serum (BSA), glucose, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfateuric was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. MTS Cell Proliferation Colorimetric Assay Kit was purchased from Promega (WI, USA). All solutions were prepared and diluted using autoclaved double-deionized.

The morphology and particle size of CDs were obtained from JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopic (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence measurements, including fluorescence intensity, time scan, and 3D fluorescence scan, were carried out on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) throughout the study. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were investigated by a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was implemented by a KAlpha1063 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Fluorescence emission images of CDs were obtained under ultraviolet excitation. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves (lifetime) of CDs were obtained by an Edinburgh Instruments FLSP920.

Firstly, 5.0 g sucrose, 5.0 mL phosphoric acid and 15.0 mL water were mixed thoroughly and kept at 80 °C for 0.8 h in thermostat water bath. Then, a certain amount of ammonium hydroxide was slowly added into the above mixture with vigorous stirring. Finally, the brown CDs solution could be obtained. Before the application of CDs, the crude products were filtered with 0.22 μm filtration membrane and dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substances. After 48 h vacuum freeze-drying, the pure CDs products were obtained and stored at 4 °C before use.

The QY of CDs was tested as previously reported [43]. The quantum yield was estimated with Eq. (1). The quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) in sulfuric acid (0.1 mol L−1, η = 1.33) was used as the standard and 360 nm as the excitation wavelength as reference.
(1)
Y
x
=
Y
s
F

A

2
F
s
A
x
η
s
2
Where F, A, and η represent the quantum yield of the standard integrated fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index, respectively. The subscripts “s” and “x” denote known fluorescent standard samples and experimental samples.

For the cell cytotoxicity investigation, MTS assay was employed to calculate cytotoxicity of CDs on MCF-7 cells. Firstly, MCF-7 cells were grown in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS. Then, MCF-7 cells were grown in medium for 24 h. After that, the cells and different CDs (0, 10, 50, 100, 150, 300,400 and 500 μg/mL) were seeded in fresh medium for incubation 24 h or 48 h. After the old medium was removed, 20 μL MTS and 100 μL fresh medium (without FBS) were added to well. Subsequently, the cells and MTS were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, the absorption of the cell solution was recorded using a microplate reader (BioTek Instrument, Inc., USA). The cell viability was estimated as follows: viability (%) = (ODp - ODb)/(ODa - ODb) × 100%, where ODa and ODp were acquired from cell solutions in the absence and presence of CDs, the ODb was blank solution without cells and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbic acid detection based on fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In ascorbic acid (AA) detection assay, different concentrations of AA solutions were first diluted. Then, different AA solutions were introduced into the CDs solution and incubation for few minutes at room template. Finally, the fluorescence signal of mixture solution was recorded. For AA detection in serum sample, the human serum sample was mixed with acetonitrile for 5 min by vortex oscillation. The mixture was then centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 20 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was blown dry with nitrogen and dissolve it with ultra-pure water. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines and approved by the Life-Science Ethics Review Committee of Xiangtan University. The detection process of AA in human serum sample was the same as performed in buffer.

In this work, the CDs were synthesized with sucrose as carbon source, phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide as the oxidant and passivator, respectively. First, the effect of element doping on the synthesis and fluorescence of CDs was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , there is no fluorescence in the solution with phosphoric acid or ammonium hydroxide, which is accord with that blank (sucrose only). An obvious fluorescent signal (emission at 356 and 440 nm) can be observed in the presence of both phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide. This result indicates that the synthesis of CDs is ascribed to the interactions of oxidant (phosphoric acid) and passivator (ammonium hydroxide). In order to further investigated effect of passivation process on the syntheses of CDs, different passivator including ethylene diamine (EDA), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), tris, ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), were explored in this reaction system. When the excitation wavelength is set at 280 nm, only the solution with NH3·H2O can induce the syntheses of CDs and show obvious emission spectra. Both the solutions with EDA and NH3·H2O have fluorescence signal when the excitation wavelength is set at 340 nm, while the NH3·H2O included solution show higher fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1).

To obtain the CDs with the better fluorescence property, the basic synthesis conditions such as reaction time and temperature were firstly optimized. As shown in Fig. S2, with increased the reaction time and temperature, the fluorescence intensity of solutions was increasing firstly and then decreasing. The hydrothermal reaction at 80 °C for 0.8 h was used in the subsequent experiment (Fig. S3). Finally, the effect of ammonium hydroxide and phosphoric acid on the fluorescence of CDs was also investigated. These results indicate that the volume ratio (N/P) of 1:6 was the optimal (Fig. S4). Thus, the volume ratio (N/P) of 1:6 heating at 80 °C for 0.8 h was selected as the optimal condition for CDs synthesis.

The size distribution and morphology of the prepared CDs were characterized by higher resolution transmission electromicroscope (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the CDs exhibit good solution dispersion and the average particle diameter is 2.0 ± 0.54 nm, which is smaller than those of other CDs [21,27]. Then, the fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was used to identify the structure and chemical bonds of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the carbon dots exhibit the typical peaks at 3205 cm−1, 1369 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1, which are contributed to the N–H and C–N bond [33,34]. The peaks at 3305 cm−1 and 1517 cm−1 are contributed to O–H and CC bond. Besides, the peak at 531.12 cm−1 are resulted from PO4 3−[ [35,36]]. These results reveal that N and P atoms were successful doped in fluorescent CDs. The element analysis and surface composition of CDs were further characterized by the XPS. All illustrated in Fig. 2d, theses CDs show five typical peaks at 285.85, 400.12, 188.8, 133.45, and 532.79 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, P2s, P2p, and O1s. The XPS analysis of the elemental composition demonstrates that these CDs were mainly includes of carbon (66.02%), oxygen (26.81%), nitrogen (4.51%), and phosphorus (2.66%). Furthermore, the N 1s spectrum of CDs was well-fitted into C–N–C (399.17eV), N–H (399.6 eV), and C–N/N–N/P–N (401.1 eV) bonds (Fig. 2e). The high-resolution P2p spectrum was well-fitted into two representative peaks at 133.9 eV and 133.1 eV, which are derived from the P–C/P–N and P–O (Fig. 2f) [37,38]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum and O 1s spectrum demonstrate the presence of bonds for CC, C–C, C–OH, CN, O–CO, PO, C–OH, P–O and so on (Fig. S5) [39]. All these results demonstrate that the as-prepared CDs include lots of –NH2 and PO4 3− groups on the surfaces, which is basically consistent with the above FT-IR analysis.

After confirmed the structure of CDs, the optical properties (UV–vis absorption and fluorescence property) of CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the CDs have clear absorption band at 280 nm and 340 nm, which might be due to π→π* transitions of CC, CN, or NP groups and n→π* electronic transitions of the CO bond, respectively [32,33]. Under UV lamp, the CDs solutions emit mazarine fluorescence (with 254 extitation) or blue fluorescence (with 310 extitation). The 3D fluorescence scan also shows two obvious excitation peak and emission peak. (280/356 nm and 340/440 nm) (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, the fluorescence property was investigated detailed. With the regular increase of excitation wavelength from 260 to 440 nm, the fluorescence emission of CDs shows an excitation independent behavior under 310 nm, while the fluorescence emission of CDs is red shift with the increased excitation wavelength (Fig. 3d). The excitation-independent fluorescent behavior is probably related to the effect of surface passivation functionality [40,41]. While excitation-dependent band-edge emission character of CDs (330–440 nm) is mainly derived from their different sizes and surface states which affect the band gad of CDs [38,39]. Moreover, the relative quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured using quinine sulfate as the reference. The QY of CDs was measured to 25.51%, which is higher than the reported CDs (Fig. S6 and Table S1) [37,38]. All these results indicate that the prepared CDs have excellent optical properties and high QY, enabling it as a nanoprobe for fluorescence analysis.

Fluorescence stability is a very important parameter for nanomaterials in practical applications. So, we studied the influence of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs both at Em = 356 nm and Em = 440 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, all the selected metal ions do not cause obvious fluorescence changes for CDs. Subsequently, fluorescent time scan of CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4c, there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity of CDs after 2 h. When CDs was stored in dark condition, it can maintain the original fluorescence even after 7 d (Fig. 4d). These results demonstrate that the as-prepared CDs have excellent fluorescence stability and can work as a promising candidate for practical applications.

Subsequently, the cytotoxicity of this CDs on cells growth was investigated. In this assay, MCF-7 cells were selected as the model cells and investigated detailedly. As shown in Fig. 5 , when the MCF-7 cells were incubated with different CDs (from 0.01 to 0.5 mg mL−1) for 24 h, there was almost no effect to the cell viability. Moreover, when the incubation time was prolonged to 48 h, the MCF-7 cells remained keep higher survival rate (95%). These results indicate that the CDs have superior biocompatibility and can be used for intracellular application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of CDs toward ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a water-soluble pharmaceutical compound and is widely used in free-radical scavenger, drug poisoning. In humans, AA deficiency will cause scurvy, and can eventually cause death [42]. However, the excessive intake of AA may lead to some other diseases, such as diarrhea and urinary stone [43]. Thus, it is significance to develop some facile and rapid methods for monitoring AA concentration. Herein, this dual-emission CDs were employed as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for AA detection due to the hydrogen bond between the AA and CDs. Firstly, the effect of pH value on the interaction between CDs and AA was investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the pH value has no significant effect on the detection system. When the pH value was 7.0, this probe exhibits highest signal-to-background ratio for AA detection. So, the pH 7.0 was chosen as the optimal detection condition. In the presence of different AA, the fluorescence intensity of CDs emission at 356 nm decreases continuously, but the emission at 440 nm exhibited negligible fluorescence changes (Fig. 6 a and b). What is more, the ratios (F356 nm/F440 nm) display a good relationship versus the AA concentration varying from 0.1 to 200 μM (Fig. 6c). The linear regression equation is expressed as y = −0.0142 x + 3.0928 (R2 = 0.9926), where y represents the fluorescence intensity ratios (F356/F440) and x represents the concentration of AA. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.85 μM based on the signal to noise ratio of S/N = 3. The selectivity assays also reveal that other biomolecules display negligible effects on the fluorescence intensities of CDs (Fig. 6d).

In order to clarify the quenching mechanism of CDs by AA, the absorbance spectrum and fluorescent lifetime of the CDs in the absence or presence of AA were carried out. As shown in Fig. S8, the absorbance peak of AA (ca. 275) overlaps with that of CDs, and the absorbance intensity is increased with the increase of AA concentrations [44]. There were no obvious changes of fluorescence lifetime on AA to CDs solutions (Fig. 7 a). Thus, the quenching mechanism of CDs by AA may be due to the inner filter effect (IFE). In order to further verify this hypothesis, corrections were made by taking into consideration the cell geometry and absorption characteristics of the aqueous solution of AA and CDs with eq (2) (Fig. 7b) [45]. These results showed that the IFE reached as high as 80% of the total suppressed efficiency of CDs (Table S3). So, the quenching mechanism of the AA to CDs may due to the IFE, which was consistent with previous work [44,46].
(2)
F
c o r
F

=

where F
obsd is the observed fluorescence intensity, F
cor is the corrected fluorescence intensity by removing IFE from F
obsd ; A
ex and A
em represent the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength (λex = 280 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λem = 356 nm), respectively; s is the thickness of excitation beam (0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm in this case), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). The maximum correction factor can be 3 at most, otherwise, the correction is not convincing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ascorbic acid in complex sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical application ability of this strategy, the 20% (v/v) human serum was employed to simulate the complex samples during testing. Firstly, the serum samples were spiked with standard AA solutions. Then, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after added the serum samples, which contained different concentrations of AA. As shown Table 1 , the recoveries of AA human serum ranged from 98.03% to 100.23%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 4.7%. These results further demonstrate the reliability and feasibility of developed ratiometric nanoprobe for AA detection in complex samples.

In summary, high-stable CDs were synthesized by a facile chemical oxidation and surface passivation in low-temperature condition. The as-prepared CDs exhibit dual emissions fluorescence (356 nm and 440 nm), high quantum yields (25.51%), and excellent fluorescent stability even under broad pH range or high ionic strengths. When cells were incubated with these fluorescent CDs, there was almost no effect to the cell viability. Moreover, this fluorescent CDs was employed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for AA detection due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between AA and CDs. Because of the hydrogen bond between the AA and the groups on CDs surface, this probe possesses high selectivity and wide linear response range for AA detection both in buffers and biological samples. Thus, we believe that this strategy for CDs synthesis in low-temperature will further contribute to the development and application of CDs. Some studies on further applications of this CDs are currently being undertaken in our laboratory.

Caicheng Long: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Taiping Qing: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Qing Fu: Data curation, Validation. Zixin Jiang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jin Xu: Investigation. Peng Zhang: Investigation. Bo Feng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777135, 51708475) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2019JJ40283, 2019JJ50596, 2018JJ3496), the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (19A501, 18C0102), and Hunan 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Engineering & Technology with Environmental Benignity and Effective Resource Utilization.
